name: BinPack
comment:
Simple bin packing for rectangles.

Based on [the version in Haskell](https://github.com/bflyblue/binpack/blob/master/Data/BinPack/R2.hs).

[The demo of a similar algorithm](https://observablehq.com/@mourner/simple-rectangle-packing).

[The article on how it works](https://codeincomplete.com/articles/bin-packing/).

Create one with `container <width> <height>` and then add rectangles and values using `pack` or `forcePack`:

    -- :: BinPack Color
    BinPack.container 300 250
        |> pack ( { width = 10, height = 30 }, Color.black )
        |> pack ( { width = 20, height = 15 }, Color.red )
        |> pack ( { width = 5, height = 25 }, Color.blue )
        |> ...

    -- :: BinPack String
    BinPack.container 300 250
        |> pack ( { width = 10, height = 30 }, "Martha" )
        |> pack ( { width = 20, height = 15 }, "Ben" )
        |> pack ( { width = 5, height = 25 }, "John" )
        |> ...

# Core type

@docs BinPack

# Create container or node

Usually you don't need creating nodes manually, prefer creating an empty container and filling it with cells using `pack`.

@docs container

# Packing

@docs pack, carelessPack, packAll

# Search

@docs find

# Folding

@docs fold, foldGeometry

# Lists

@docs toList

# Mapping

@docs map

unions.name: BinPack
unions.comment: `BinPack a`, where `a` is the type of what every rectangle is associated with (what lies in every cell). For example, it could be `Color`.

values.name: carelessPack
values.comment: Try to pack the value in a rectangle with given width and height. If the rectangle doesn't fit, ignore that fact and return previous condition of `BinPack`.
values.type: ( { width : Basics.Float, height : Basics.Float }, a ) -> BinPack.BinPack a -> BinPack.BinPack a

values.name: container
values.comment: Create an empty container with given height and width.

values.name: find
values.comment: Try to find a value in a structure using given coordinates.
values.type: { x : Basics.Float, y : Basics.Float } -> BinPack.BinPack a -> Maybe.Maybe ( a, Bounds.Bounds )

values.name: fold
values.comment:
Fold the `BinPack a` to any other type, for example:

    BinPack.container 300 250
        |> pack ( { width = 10, height = 30 }, Color.black )
        |> pack ( { width = 20, height = 15 }, Color.red )
        |> pack ( { width = 5, height = 25 }, Color.blue )
        |> fold (::) []

    -- == [ Color.black, Color.red, Color.blue ]
values.type: (a -> b -> b) -> b -> BinPack.BinPack a -> b

values.name: foldGeometry
values.comment:
Fold the structure, using both the values and their bounds:

    BinPack.container 20 100
        |> pack ( { width = 10, height = 30 }, Color.black )
        |> pack ( { width = 20, height = 15 }, Color.red )
        |> pack ( { width = 5, height = 25 }, Color.blue )
        |> pack ( { width = 12, height = 25 }, Color.green )
        |> foldByBounds (::) []

    ==
        [ ( Color.black, { x = 0, y = 0, width = 10, height = 30 } )
        , ( Color.red, { x = 0, y = 30, width = 20, height = 15 } )
        , ( Color.blue, { x = 10, y = 0, width = 5, height = 25 } )
        , ( Color.green, { x = 0, y = 45, width = 12, height = 25 } )
        ]
values.type: (( a, Bounds.Bounds ) -> k -> k) -> k -> BinPack.BinPack a -> k

values.name: map
values.comment: Substitute all of the cells with different ones, using the previous ones as the source.

values.name: pack
values.comment: Try to pack the value in a rectangle with given width and height. If the rect doesn't fit, `Nothing` is returned.
values.type: ( { width : Basics.Float, height : Basics.Float }, a ) -> BinPack.BinPack a -> Maybe.Maybe (BinPack.BinPack a)

values.name: packAll
values.comment: Try to pack all the values with given dimensions in a `BinPack` container with given width and height, ignore the item when it doesn't fit.
values.type: Basics.Float -> Basics.Float -> List.List ( { width : Basics.Float, height : Basics.Float }, a ) -> BinPack.BinPack a

values.name: toList
values.comment: Convert the structure to the list of values and their bounds
